question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the uplift for Direct Payments to carers for 2022-23 will be announced.
answer_text: I was pleased to announce an uplift of £7.1m for 2022-23 for Direct Payments on 6 October, following a visit to meet personal assistants and their employers at the Centre for Independent Living. In real terms, this should see Personal Assistants receiving an uplift of around 12% in their hourly rate.The funding will allow individuals who employ personal assistant carers via Direct Payments to offer improved rates of pay and in turn support the continued provision of care in the context of an increasingly competitive employment market, including in the social care sector.Direct payments are an extremely important part of our care system, particularly in allowing for the personalisation of care. I know that many people value the control and flexibility they can facilitate, by letting them arrange and pay for their own care and support services, and employing their own carers directly.Personal assistants employed through Self-Directed Support play a vital role in enhancing the quality of life for the disabled person they are employed by and I am pleased to be able to recognise the value they bring to both individuals, and the wider Health and Social care system, by this £7.1m upliftWhilst we continue to operate in a very challenging environment, I am confident that this funding package will support the continued provision of care through direct payments. It further underlines my commitment to supporting a person-centred approach, whilst ensuring that people’s assessed care needs are fulfilled.While I have no doubt this additional funding will be very welcome, I have to again put on record the unsustainable nature of the budgetary situation my Department is facing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the programme of works for the Eastern Division's 2022-2023 financial year will be completed and distributed to MLAs.
answer_text: My Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation has recently been confirmed and our work programmes for 2022/23 are being finalised accordingly.  When finalised, details of the proposed works programmes will be included in the Department’s 2022/23 Council Reports, which will be presented to the respective councils during the summer months and published thereafter.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of vacancies relative to the capacity of the Whiterock Day Centre.
answer_text: There is currently one vacancy of a band 5 Support Worker. The recruitment process for this post is currently ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much it costs his Department to maintain the Whiterock Day Centre on an annual basis.
answer_text: Staffing for the Whiterock Day Centre is provided by the North Belfast Day Centre.  The non-pay budget for Whiterock, which covers costs such as staff training, staff mileage and stationary, is £8,640 per annum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what health services are currently being provided at the Whiterock Day Centre, Whiterock Road.
answer_text: Whiterock Mental Health Day Centre is open two days per week and provides therapeutic, practical and creative support to meet the needs of adults aged 18 – 65 years with mental ill health. The Day Centre provides a range of services, including Health Promotion Programmes, Self-esteem, confidence building, Holistic Therapies, Creative Activities, Physical Health Programmes, Life Skills and Horticultural Therapies Mindfulness.Interventions are individualised, person centred and provided for a time-limited period based on assessed need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when NI Water will address the insufficient capacity in the West Belfast area and what work is being undertaken to manage capacity.
answer_text: Work undertaken to manage and address sewerage capacity in the West Belfast area and across the North is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what immeadiate measures his Department is taking to address health inequalities in the Blackmountain Ward of west Belfast.
answer_text: As shown in the most recent Health Inequalities Annual Report for NI, the Black Mountain District Electoral Area (DEA) had worse health outcomes than the NI average for 33 of the 43 indicators analysed.Making Life Better (MLB) is the Executive’s overarching strategic framework to improve health and address health inequalities. It was developed based on the evidence that health and wellbeing, and health inequalities, are shaped by many factors including age, family, community, workplace, beliefs and traditions, economics, and physical and social environments. Many of these are outside the control of the Department of Health.Reducing inequalities in health is a significant challenge. Through MLB, and across a range of key issues, my Department continues to collaborate across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally.We are also working with colleagues from the Public Health Agency to further build community development capacity and approaches within Northern Ireland. Community development is a long-term, value-based process which aims to redress imbalance and bring about change founded on social justice, equality, and inclusion.Belfast Trust is also committed to improving health and well-being and reducing health and social care inequalities. Their Equality Scheme clearly sets out what they do in their daily work to comply with their Section 75 dual responsibilities to promote equality of opportunity and to promote good relations.Belfast Trust has an Equality Action Plan, Disability Action Plan and Good Relations Strategy to promote equality of opportunity and good relations and in turn tackle inequalities and make a real positive difference to the lives or experiences of our service users, carers and staff. Belfast Trust is currently drafting their next Equality Action Plan, Disability Action Plan and Good Relations Strategy which will run from 2023-2028.Belfast Trust is continuing to deliver its social prescribing service - Connected Community Care.  Each area has a Wellbeing Co-ordinator who completes a holistic needs assessment with individuals referred to the service to support their health and wellbeing including housing, finance, diet, exercise, smoking cessation and loneliness and isolation.  They are then connected into services within the community and voluntary sector to address these needs.  It is also the role of the Co-ordinator to identify gaps in services, and find solutions to address these gaps and build capacity within the local area.I also recently participated in engagement with my Ministerial colleagues on a Four Nations basis on this topic, as health inequalities are a challenge faced right across the UK. There is a shared commitment to tackling this issue as we emerge from the COVID pandemic and respond to the rising cost of living. There is already significant work underway

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what additional measures his Department is putting in place to accommodate staff, with a working pattern of 8am to 8pm, who have had their parking permits removed.
answer_text: The Department’s policy on car parking provision and management in the Health and Social Care (HSC) sector for Northern Ireland states that it is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine the way that the car parking policy is applied to each of their sites, to enable them to take into account local issues.Staff parking permits are not issued by the NHSCT, SHSCT or WHSCT and the SEHSCT have not removed any staff parking permits.The BHSCT does not have sufficient car parking spaces to meet the demands for all staff and have introduced a new application, assessment, verification and allocation process to ensure that available car parking spaces are allocated on the basis of business need, while also considering the availability to staff of alternative travel options. The criteria applied recognises the large number of staff who work shifts; commencing early in the day and finishing late into the evening.Staff, who do not meet the criteria for a parking permit, have the right to an independent appeal and can also request a personal travel plan which identifies their available travel options, based on their home and work locations and their shift pattern.
